title: Conformance Known-Answer Vector File
description: Schema for conformance/vectors/*.json. NON-NORMATIVE: it validates testing artifacts, not CDX documents. Its job is to catch corruption or typos in a file that third parties consume, so the constraints are deliberately tight.

clause.description: The specification section these vectors pin.

oracle.description: How the expected values were derived. Required because a vector whose expectations came from the implementation under test proves nothing — it snapshots a bug instead of catching it.

requires.description: Capability keys (capabilities.json) that EVERY vector in this file needs — the file exercises a feature gated on them (e.g. the security extension, or provenance verification). Merged with each vector's own `requires`. Absent means the file is pure core and always in scope.

allOf.$comment: A vector asserts a value or a rejection, never both: expectReject is mutually exclusive with expectedCanonicalJcs.

local.description: MUST NOT appear: `local` is this-implementation-only data and is stripped at the export boundary. Its presence means the boundary leaked.

expectedCanonicalJcs.description: The RFC 8785 (JCS) byte string a faithful canonicalizer MUST produce. Present on a transform vector; absent on a reject vector.

expectReject.description: Marks a vector whose input a conformant implementation MUST reject (the operation errors). Used where no portable defect code exists — the assertion is rejection, not an identifier. Mutually exclusive with expectedCanonicalJcs.

selection.description: A presentation-selection case: a DETERMINISTIC selection rule (Presentation Layers §4.3, §8.2). `breakpoint`: given breakpoints + a viewport width, the active breakpoint name (greatest minWidth wins; a minWidth tie goes to the later array entry; bounds inclusive; omitted bound unbounded). `default`: given a candidate presentation list, the index of the default (first `default:true`, else the first entry). The §4.3 target-narrowing is advisory and not modelled.

breakpoints.description: breakpoint rule: the responsive breakpoints in declared order.

width.description: breakpoint rule: the viewport width to resolve.

candidates.description: default rule: the candidate presentation entries in `manifest.presentation[]` order.

name.description: breakpoint rule: the winning breakpoint name, or null if none match.

index.description: default rule: the index of the selected candidate.

anchor.description: An anchor-offset case: a block's text content and a {start,end} range. Anchor offsets are CODE POINTS (Unicode scalar values), not UTF-16 code units (Anchors and References §3), so an astral character counts as one — a UTF-16 implementation using string indexing mis-targets the selection.

text.description: The block's text content (the concatenation of its text-node values).

expectedSelection.description: The substring the [start,end) code-point range selects.

structural.description: A structural-constraints case: a content-tree / file-level invariant JSON Schema cannot express. The adapter runs the named rule's checker over `instance` and reports whether it FLAGGED; the suite compares that to `expect.valid`. The case is self-describing — it ships the block-type recognition its outcome depends on, so no schema-derived state is needed.

allOf.$comment: Block-tree rules need the root context and the block-type recognition their outcome depends on; omitting either would silently mis-decide the case.
asset-index-consistency compares a manifest category (instance) against its index.

rule.description: Which structural rule to run — also the portable pass/fail discriminator.

instance.description: The primary input: a block tree (containment/cardinality), an anchor node/string (anchor-range), a precise-layout node (page-number-integrity), the manifest asset CATEGORY object (asset-index-consistency), or an items array (id-uniqueness).

index.description: asset-index-consistency only: the asset INDEX object (its `assets[]`). Ignored by other rules.

root.description: Block-tree rules only: the root context. The upward-containment exemption keys off `excerpt`, so the same instance can be valid at excerpt root and invalid at document root — it MUST be explicit.

blockTypes.description: Block-tree rules only: the `type` strings the adapter MUST treat as content blocks when walking `instance`. Shipped in the vector (not derived from a schema) because the fire/clean outcome depends on which nodes count as blocks — e.g. a tableCell under a paragraph fires only because `paragraph` is recognized, giving a concrete non-excerpt parent.

valid.description: `true`: a conformant reader accepts the instance (the rule does NOT flag it). `false`: the instance violates the rule (it MUST flag).

robustness.description: A canonicalize-robustness case: GENERATIVE (no literal deep structure, which could itself exceed a parser's own limits). The adapter expands it relative to ITS OWN declared canonicalization depth bound, so the case is portable across implementations with different bounds.

part.description: Which part carries the deep structure.

boundOffset.description: Nest `of` to (the adapter's declared max canonicalization depth) + boundOffset, measured at the part's baseline. `boundOffset: 1` is one level past the bound and MUST reject with a catchable error (never a crash); `0` is the exact bound. The range is kept small so a generated depth stays near the bound on every implementation — a large literal depth could overflow a recursive-descent JSON parser UPSTREAM of the depth guard, turning a catchable rejection into an uncatchable crash. NOTE: a `metadata` term sits two levels below the root (dublinCore → terms → term), so its effective absolute depth is 2 higher than a `content` case at the same boundOffset — a `metadata` accept needs boundOffset ≤ -2.

of.description: The innermost scalar the nesting wraps. Restricted to a scalar so a case cannot smuggle a literal deep structure in via `of`, defeating the generative design.

expect.description: `accept`: canonicalization succeeds. `reject`: it fails with a CATCHABLE error — a typed rejection the adapter reports, not a native stack overflow that crashes it.

requires.description: Capability keys (from capabilities.json) this vector needs. An adapter that does not declare all of them has the vector SKIPPED — reported explicitly, never counted as a pass. Absent means the vector is pure core and always in scope.

severity.description: Normative weight of this vector. Absent defaults to MUST: a failure is fatal. SHOULD failures are advisory and never fatal.
